chunk_summary: Chronic osteomyelitis with sequestrum and sinus tract disease requires surgical debridement, long-term antibiotics, and orthopedic follow-up to manage infection, prevent complications, and address relapse risks.
learning_objectives: Understand the pathophysiology and clinical presentation of chronic osteomyelitis with sequestrum and sinus tract disease.
Identify appropriate imaging modalities and their role in defining the extent of infection.
Learn surgical and antibiotic management strategies for chronic osteomyelitis.
Recognize potential long-term complications, including growth arrest and limb deformities.
key_facts: Chronic osteomyelitis often follows inadequately treated acute infection or direct inoculation.
Imaging findings include sclerosis, cortical thickening, lytic destruction, sequestrum, and sinus formation.
Management includes surgical debridement and 6-12 weeks of antibiotics, sometimes longer if hardware remains.
Operative cultures, not superficial swabs, should guide antibiotic therapy.
Long-term orthopedic follow-up is essential to monitor for complications like growth arrest or limb-length discrepancies.
reference_quote: Management begins with defining the full extent of necrotic bone, hardware involvement, and soft-tissue tracts using radiography with either magnetic resonance imaging or computed tomography as needed. (Indian Academy of Pediatrics Consensus Statement)
